Which is FACT, not opinion?
choices: Cats make better pets than dogs
Anyone wanting low-maintenance should choose cat
questions.explanation: Cat sounds CAN be measured against dog sounds.
questions.passageText: Cats make better pets than dogs. They are quieter, cleaner, and don't need to be walked twice a day. Cats can be left home alone for a few days, while dogs cannot. They use a litter box, so you don't have to take them outside in the rain. Anyone who wants a low-maintenance pet should choose a cat.

questions.question: What is the main idea of the whole passage?
choices: Humans have used wind for ages, and now use it to make electricity
questions.passageText:
Wind has been put to work by humans for thousands of years. Ancient sailors used wind to push boats across oceans. Farmers used windmills to grind grain.

Today, wind is used to make electricity. Tall towers called wind turbines have blades that spin in the wind. The spinning powers a generator that creates electricity. The electricity is sent through wires to homes and businesses.

Wind is a clean energy source — it doesn't pollute the air or water. But it has limits. Wind doesn't blow all the time. So we still need other energy sources to fill the gaps. Still, wind power is growing fast around the world.

questions.question: What is the main idea of the passage?
choices: Lighthouses guide ships using flashing patterns of light
questions.explanation: Covers purpose AND how they work (patterns, light).

wrongExplanations.3: WRONG — passage says some don't anymore.

questions.passageText:
Lighthouses have guided ships for thousands of years. Their job is simple: warn sailors that land or rocks are nearby, especially at night or in fog.

A lighthouse uses a powerful light at the top of a tall tower. The light flashes in a special pattern — for example, three quick flashes, then a pause. Each lighthouse has its own pattern, so sailors know which lighthouse they are seeing.

Long ago, lighthouses used fires or oil lamps. Today, they use electric bulbs and computers. Some lighthouses have no keepers anymore — they run on their own.

questions.question: Best topic sentence for paragraph about saving energy at home:
choices: My dad pays the electric bill.
Saving energy at home cuts costs and helps the environment.

questions.explanation: Paragraph 2 explains the flash pattern signaling system.
questions.passageText:
Lighthouses have guided ships for thousands of years. Their job is simple: warn sailors that land or rocks are nearby, especially at night or in fog.

A lighthouse uses a powerful light at the top of a tall tower. The light flashes in a special pattern — for example, three quick flashes, then a pause. Each lighthouse has its own pattern, so sailors know which lighthouse they are seeing.

Long ago, lighthouses used fires or oil lamps. Today, they use electric bulbs and computers. Some lighthouses have no keepers anymore — they run on their own.

questions.question: What can you infer about the team's likely play?
choices: They'll play with focus, not panic
questions.passageText: Coach Rivera watched the scoreboard. Down by two with 30 seconds left. He called a timeout. The team huddled around him, sweating, breathing hard. "I want everyone to know," he said quietly, "that no matter what happens in the next 30 seconds, I'm proud of every one of you. You've worked harder than any team I've coached." Marcus looked up. He had expected a strategy talk. Instead, he felt suddenly calm. The team broke the huddle and walked back onto the court.

questions.question: What can you INFER about why Tubman traveled at night?
choices: Slave catchers couldn't see in the dark
She used the North Star to navigate
questions.passageText: Harriet Tubman is the most famous conductor of the Underground Railroad. After escaping slavery in 1849, she returned to the South 13 times to lead approximately 70 people to freedom. She traveled at night, following the North Star, and used songs with hidden messages. Despite a $40,000 reward for her capture, she was never caught and never lost a single passenger. The journey was extremely dangerous — escapees walked 10 to 20 miles between stations, faced slave catchers with dogs, and traveled in freezing temperatures.

Which is OPINION, not fact?
choices: A few uniform sets can last all year
This trend is the most positive change
questions.passageText:
Many schools require uniforms. This trend is one of the most positive changes in education today, and every school should consider adopting it.

Uniforms reduce bullying based on clothing brands. They save families money. Most importantly, they help students focus on learning. Schools should embrace this simple change.

Today, wind is used to make electricity. Tall towers called wind turbines have blades that spin in the wind. The spinning powers a generator that creates electricity. The electricity is sent through wires to homes and businesses.

Wind is a clean energy source — it doesn't pollute the air or water. But it has limits. Wind doesn't blow all the time. So we still need other energy sources to fill the gaps. Still, wind power is growing fast around the world.

questions.question: What is the author's purpose?
choices: Persuade school boards to extend recess
questions.explanation: "School boards need to make recess longer" = persuade.
questions.passageText: Schools today give kids only 15 or 20 minutes of recess. That's not enough. Studies show that kids who have at least 30 minutes of recess each day do better in math and reading. They also have fewer behavior problems. School boards across the country need to make recess longer. Our kids' health and learning depend on it.

questions.question: How can you infer Marcus felt during the four days?
questions.explanation: Barely eating, waking at 3am, posters everywhere = worry.
questions.passageText: Marcus put up posters all over the neighborhood. "LOST DOG: Buddy. Brown lab. Last seen Tuesday." Three days passed with no calls. Marcus barely ate. He woke up at 3am thinking he heard barking. On the fourth night, the phone rang. "I think I have your dog," a woman said. Marcus drove to her house with shaking hands. When the front door opened, Buddy ran out, wagging his tail wildly. Marcus dropped to his knees and buried his face in the dog's fur. He couldn't speak.

questions.question: Which is OPINION?
questions.explanation: "Distract" is the author's view (others would disagree).
questions.passageText: Tablets are everywhere in schools today. Some say they're the future. But tablets distract students. Kids click around to games when they should be working. Tablets break and need expensive repairs. They reduce face-to-face interaction. We've replaced books with screens — and we shouldn't have. Bring back books in classrooms.

questions.question: The ______ child cried when she ______ her favorite toy.
What can you infer Riley is feeling?
questions.explanation: Memory of grandmother + the lonely note = nostalgia/grief.

wrongExplanations.0: PARTIAL — the dust isn't the focus.

questions.passageText: Riley walked through the old house her grandmother had left her. Dust covered everything. The floorboards creaked. In the corner sat an old piano, missing a few keys. She sat down on the bench. Her grandmother had played this piano. Riley remembered being small, sitting beside her, watching her hands move across the keys. She pressed one key. It still made a sound — a single, lonely note that filled the empty house.

wrongExplanations.2: Cover is on the OUTSIDE of a magazine.

questions.explanation: MOST = more than half. So PROBABLY.

wrongExplanations.0: Facts don't compare Stowe to other mountains.

FERTILE : BARREN ::
questions.explanation: FERTILE (full of life/growth) and BARREN (empty/no life) are opposites. FULL and EMPTY are opposites.

wrongExplanations.1: Dry and wet are opposites — but full/empty more closely captures the idea.
wrongExplanations.2: Hot and warm are degrees of same.
wrongExplanations.3: Large and big mean the same.

questions.question: What does paragraph 1 mainly explain?
questions.explanation: Paragraph 1 explains tectonic plates causing earthquakes.
questions.passageText:
Earthquakes happen when huge sections of the Earth's crust suddenly slip past each other. The crust is made of giant pieces called tectonic plates, which are always moving slowly. Sometimes they get stuck. When they finally slip, the energy released causes the ground to shake.

Most earthquakes are too small to feel. But powerful earthquakes can damage buildings, break roads, and even cause tsunamis. Scientists called seismologists study earthquakes to understand them better.

While we cannot prevent earthquakes, we can prepare. Buildings in earthquake zones are designed to sway without falling. Schools practice 'drop, cover, and hold on' drills. These efforts save lives.

questions.question: What can you infer about Ravi?
questions.explanation: Sympathetic shrug = caring response to friend.
questions.passageText: Jake walked into class and froze. There was a piece of paper on every desk. "You all studied last night, right?" Mrs. Hayes said cheerfully. Jake's stomach dropped. He had been at his cousin's birthday all evening — he hadn't even opened his book. He glanced at his best friend Ravi, who gave him a sympathetic shrug. As Mrs. Hayes handed out the test, Jake saw the topic at the top: World War II. They had spent two weeks on this. He had taken notes in class every day. Maybe — just maybe — he could do this. He picked up his pencil.
